trajectory_summary: The call moves from warm to booked without ever building real energy. Randy completes the procedural checklist competently — address, credit, utility, roof, bill collection, appointment time — but walks past every emotional signal Stacy offers. The appointment is real but fragile: Stacy's prior evaluation showing minimal savings is unaddressed, her financial strain was acknowledged but never bridged to solar value, and her mother as co-title-holder is unresolved for the appointment.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked in-person appointment for the following morning at 10AM. The v1 tag is unknown, but the outcome is a conditional booking — real but structurally vulnerable. The closer will face the prior-evaluation objection cold, the mother's absence from the appointment is unresolved, and the bill collection is deferred to email rather than completed on the call.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Vulnerability disclosure — empathy bridge before procedure
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'm struggling with that right now, and that's one of the reasons why I wanna save on electricity is the financial strain is difficult.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Stacy handed Randy the emotional core of her motivation and he responded with 'I gotcha. But No problem.' and moved to the next checkbox. This is the call's highest-leverage miss: the customer disclosed financial hardship, which is both a trust gift and the exact pain solar addresses. The manager should play this clip in the 1-on-1 and ask Randy what he heard. The coaching goal is not a scripted response — it's building Randy's recognition that when a customer discloses vulnerability, procedure pauses for one human beat before resuming. Per coaching_philosophy.empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure, this is a trust gift, not an interruption.

key_moments.quote: I've actually had my house and I have a tiny house, and I had it evaluated years ago. And it basically came down to, like, there's would wouldn't be that much saving, but I don't know if things have changed.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a pre-emptive version of the home_disqualified objection — Stacy is telling Randy she has already been told solar won't work well for her. The codex's canonical response is 'Do you know why it was disqualified?' Randy never asks it. He pivots to address confirmation without acknowledging the disclosure. Strategic read: Randy may have been planning to let the specialist handle this. Reactive read: Randy didn't register it as an objection requiring a response. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a later setup move.

key_moments.quote: I think the city of Denver offers some financial assistance to get solar panels, so I'm considering that as well.
key_moments.stewart_read: Stacy is actively considering a competing program — city-subsidized solar — and Randy does not acknowledge it at all. This is a competitive alternative that will be live in the closer's conversation. The codex's comparison_framing strategy ('it's always best to get a couple looks') applies here, but Randy never deploys it. The closer will encounter this without knowing it was raised.

key_moments.quote: I'm struggling with that right now, and that's one of the reasons why I wanna save on electricity is the financial strain is difficult.
key_moments.stewart_read: Stacy discloses active financial hardship — not as an objection but as a trust gift explaining her motivation. Randy's response is 'I gotcha. But No problem.' — procedural acknowledgment with no empathy and no bridge to how solar specifically addresses financial strain. Per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion, the flip available here is: 'That's exactly why this matters — solar replaces the bill you're already paying.' Randy makes the safe move instead.

key_moments.quote: In the long term, I just got an electric car back in September, and I don't have a charger here at my house because I don't have a garage. But at some point, I would like to add that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Stacy volunteers an EV and a future charging need unprompted. This is a load-expansion signal — her electricity consumption will increase materially when she adds a charger. It also signals long-term tenure and investment intent (she said she's keeping the house forever). Randy redirects correctly to the specialist for the technical question, but he does not anchor on the enthusiasm: 'You've got an EV and you're planning to charge at home — that actually makes solar even more valuable for you specifically.' The redirect is right; the energy capture is the miss.
